coredata.dc:title: Gut microbiome interactions with graphene based nanomaterials: Challenges and opportunities

Srivastava, Manish
coredata.dc:description: Rapid growth of nanotechnology has accelerated immense possibility of engineered nanomaterials (ENMs) exposure by human and living organisms. In this context, wide range applications of graphene based nanomaterials (GBNMs) may inevitably cause their release into the environment. Consequently, potential risks to the ecological system and human health is consistently increasing due to the probable ingestion of GBNMs by mean of contaminated water or food sources. Further, gut microbiome is known to play a profound impact on the health status of human being and has been recognized as the most exciting advancement in the biomedical science. Recent studies has shown vital role of ENMs to alter gut microbiome and thereby changed pathological status of organisms. Therefore, in this review results of numerous studies dedicated to explore the impact of GBNMs on gut microbiome and thereby various pathological status have been summarized. Dietary exposure of different types of GBNMs [e.g. graphene, graphene oxide (GO), partially reduced graphene oxide (PRGO), graphene quantum dots (GQDs)] have been evaluated on the gut microbiome through numerous in vitro and in vivo models. Moreover, emphasis has been made to evaluate different physiological responses with the short/long-term exposure of GBNMs, particularly in gastrointestinal tract (GIT) and its correlation with gut microbiome and the health status. It is reviewed that exposure of GBNMs can exert significant impact which alter the composition, diversity and function of gut microbiome. This may further appear in terms of enteric disorder along with numerous pathological changes e.g. IEC (intestinal epithelial cells) colitis, lysosomal dysfunction, inflammation, shortened colon, resorbed embryo, retardation in skeletal development, low weight of fetus, early or late dead of fetus and IBD (inflammatory bowel disease) like symptoms. Finally, potential health risks due to the exposure of GBNMs have been discussed with future perspective.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Rapid growth of nanotechnology has accelerated immense possibility of engineered nanomaterials (ENMs) exposure by human and living organisms. In this context, wide range applications of graphene based nanomaterials (GBNMs) may inevitably cause their release into the environment. Consequently, potential risks to the ecological system and human health is consistently increasing due to the probable ingestion of GBNMs by mean of contaminated water or food sources. Further, gut microbiome is known to play a profound impact on the health status of human being and has been recognized as the most exciting advancement in the biomedical science. Recent studies has shown vital role of ENMs to alter gut microbiome and thereby changed pathological status of organisms. Therefore, in this review results of numerous studies dedicated to explore the impact of GBNMs on gut microbiome and thereby various pathological status have been summarized. Dietary exposure of different types of GBNMs [e.g. graphene, graphene oxide (GO), partially reduced graphene oxide (PRGO), graphene quantum dots (GQDs)] have been evaluated on the gut microbiome through numerous in vitro and in vivo models. Moreover, emphasis has been made to evaluate different physiological responses with the short/long-term exposure of GBNMs, particularly in gastrointestinal tract (GIT) and its correlation with gut microbiome and the health status. It is reviewed that exposure of GBNMs can exert significant impact which alter the composition, diversity and function of gut microbiome. This may further appear in terms of enteric disorder along with numerous pathological changes e.g. IEC (intestinal epithelial cells) colitis, lysosomal dysfunction, inflammation, shortened colon, resorbed embryo, retardation in skeletal development, low weight of fetus, early or late dead of fetus and IBD (inflammatory bowel disease) like symptoms. Finally, potential health risks due to the exposure of GBNMs have been discussed with future perspective.

ce:section.ce:para: Last decade has seen a remarkable growth of nanotechnology. Global market of nanotechnology has been analyzed over USD 3 trillion, and it has shared nearly 6 million jobs around the world by 2020 (Pandey and Jain, 2020; Q. Yang et al., 2020). Nanotechnology is emerging as a powerful scientific discipline and its growth has been noted nearly in every field of our contemporary society (Sharma et al., 2021). Further, implementations of nanomaterials are significantly changing a range of disciplines including biomedical applications (Mabrouk et al., 2021), many consumer products (e.g. electronics, sunscreens, and cosmetics) (He and Bi, 2019), agriculture and food products (Thiruvengadam et al., 2018), environmental remediation and ecological applications (Frenzilli, 2020). Emerging researches have explored that particles having size at least one dimension ranging from 1 to 100 nm known as nanomaterials exhibit unique physicochemical properties compared to their bulk forms, and therefore can be of great importance for advanced applications (Baig et al., 2021). Due to the vast applications of nanomaterials in numerous commercial products and their exposure to human and other organisms, concerns about the potential health risk are increasing day by day (Naseer et al., 2018; Khan et al., 2019). Exposure of nanoparticles is possible through different routes, and this may include oral, dermal and inhalation (Malakar et al., 2021; Warheit, 2018).

Among different types of nanomaterials, carbon-based nanostructures are highly demanding owing to their advanced applications in many fields (Oseghe et al., 2021; Riley and Narayan, 2021; Zhou et al., 2020). Further, among variety of carbon allotropes e.g. carbon nanotubes, fullerene and nanodiamond; graphene has received much attention owing to its exceptional physicochemical properties (Si et al., 2021; Abdelhalim et al., 2022). Graphene is a two dimensional one atom thick planar nanostructure, and has emerged as a sensational carbon nanomaterial. In this nanostructure, carbon atoms are sp2 bonded which are arranged in a hexagonal honeycomb lattice (Kornilov and Gubin, 2020). During last decade, interest in graphene based materials has been exponentially rising towards real applications. The global market of graphene has been analyzed to USD 94.4 million in 2020 whereas; it is likely to grow by 43.2% (compound annual growth) from 2021 to 2028 [https://www.grandviewresearch.com/industry-analysis/graphene-industry]. Graphene based materials exhibit distinctive physicochemical characteristics which make them exceptional for various technological and biomedical applications (Farjadian et al., 2020). Implementations of graphene based nanostructures have been widely explored for a number of applications e.g. energy storage (Olabi et al., 2021), electronic gadgets (J. Yang et al., 2019), food packaging (Ahmed, 2019), biosensing (Prattis et al., 2021), drug delivery (Daniyal et al., 2020), and tissue engineering (Geetha Bai et al., 2019). Nevertheless, ubiquitous applications of graphene based materials are of great concern owing to their inevitable release into the natural environments (Fadeel et al., 2018; Serrano-Luján et al., 2019). Particularly, their release in to the aquatic ecosystems and atmosphere can pose potential risks to human, animal and other living organisms (Zhao et al., 2021). Exposure of graphene based nanostructures is possible through inhalation, ingestion, and skin contact (Lovén et al., 2021; Rhazouani et al., 2021). Among these, exposure through inhalation is regarded as one of the most relevant ways, and therefore numerous in vitro and in vivo studies have been reported on this aspect (Pelin et al., 2018). Thus, potential health risk should be carefully analyzed within organisms due to the exposure of graphene based nanostructures via toxicological and other pathological measurements systematically (L. Chen et al., 2020).

Gut microbiome is known as the essential organ and potentially interacts with its host (Fan and Pedersen, 2021). A number of activities e.g. nutrient productions, metabolic reactions, epithelial growth and overall immune system are governed by microbiome (Ding et al., 2019). Therefore, gut microbiome has been regarded to play a key role to determine the overall health status of the human body. Further, numerous studies have revealed that exposure to different chemicals and nanomaterials can directly or indirectly alter the compositions and diversity of the microbiome (Lamas et al., 2020; Chiu et al., 2020). Unintentional ingestion of graphene based materials is possible through the contaminated water body or food (also from food packaging) when they are released through different waste products or industries related to graphene based materials. Consequently, pathological properties of GIT can also be affected with the interaction of graphene based materials, and this may further influence the diversity and functionality of microbiome (Tang et al., 2021). It has also been reported that the growth and kinetics of bacterial species relevant to gut microbiome can be significantly influenced with the exposure of variety of nanomaterials (Zhang et al., 2020). Alteration in the bacterial microbiome can lead to GI homeostasis that may be associated with the perturbed immune system and declined ability to fight against the pathogenesis (Abbasi et al., 2018). In spite of great interest about the graphene based nanostructures for their vast applications, deep understanding on pros and cons in context to biocompatibility and toxicity of graphene and its derivatives are the matter of great concern (Rajakumari et al., 2020; Achawi et al., 2021). Therefore, it is quite necessary to evaluate the fate and possible toxicological effects of graphene and its derivatives on living organisms critically (Tabish et al., 2018; Montagner et al., 2016).

In view of above issues, this review discuss about numerous studies that have been reported in the context of graphene based nanostructures and their influence on gut microbiome. Emphases have been given on the numerous aspects including influence of different types of graphene based nanostructures on the diversity, composition and diversity of gut microbiome using in vitro and in vivo models. In addition, results of the pharmacokinetics studies, physiological parameters, toxicological and antibacterial effect, biocompatibility, and possible applications of graphene based nanostructures as therapeutic agent in reference to the gut microbiome and GIT have been evaluated. Conclusion and future prospects have been summarized in reference to the interaction of GBNMs with gut microbiome and their consequence on the potential health issues.

Graphene can be defined as a single layer of 3D graphite structure. It is a 2D monolayer sheet wherein carbon atoms are sp2 bonded, and are arranged in a tightly packed honeycomb lattice. In this structure, atoms are connected with a bond length of 1.42 Å whereas; pilling of graphene sheets together with an inter-planar distance of 3.35 Å forms the 3D graphite structure (Mbayachi et al., 2021). Furthermore, in graphene, carbon atoms are covalently bonded together within the plane and forms σ-bonds with three neighboring carbon atoms whereas one out-of the plane forms π-bond. Such chemical structure makes graphene extraordinary to exhibit unique physicochemical properties in terms of specific surface area, intrinsic ionic mobility, electronic conductivity, thermal conductivity, optical transmittance and mechanical strength. Graphene derivatives include a wide range of materials which may be distinct in numerous aspects e.g. thickness, size, chemical compositions and degree of oxidation. The common examples of graphene derivatives are GO, RGO and GQDs. Generally, in reference to the chemical composition, when oxygen content is 0 at.% having 2D-monolayer it is known as graphene. On the other hand, with composition of oxygen content ~50 at.%, it can be referred to GO. Furthermore, conversion of GO into RGO, PRGO can be done with the removal of certain oxygen containing functional groups, wherein carbon to oxygen ratio may be find in the range of 8:1 to 246:1 (Lee et al., 2019; Shang et al., 2015). The reduction process can be performed following diverse physical and chemical methods (Lee et al., 2019). Besides, GBNMs having size below 10 nm can be termed as GQDs, exhibit prominent quantum confinement and therefore excellent optical properties (Munuera et al., 2021). In general, ideal GQDs should be consisting of monolayer of carbon atoms, but in most of the cases, prepared GQDs may compose multilayers along with oxygen and hydrogen based functional. Additionally, depending upon the number of layers, graphene can also be designated to multi layer graphene (MLGR) and few layer graphene (FLGR). A number of characterizations techniques can be employed to probe the physicochemical properties of GBNMs. Particularly, high resolution scanning electron (HR-TEM) and Raman spectroscopy can be very effective to analyze the properties of GBNMs (Figs. 1 and 2
) (Abbas et al., 2020; Somanathan et al., 2015; Fazi et al., 2020; Haidari et al., 2020; Scardaci and Compagnini, 2021; Sahoo et al., 2011).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Gut microbiome exposure with GBNMs: potential harmful effect
ce:section.ce:para: A strong correlation between the gut microbiome and the immunity/health of living organisms have been extensively studied (Lamas et al., 2020; Zheng et al., 2020). In this context, it is very possible that the ingestion of GBNMs can directly influence the intestinal immunity of living organism. These phenomena can be associated with the modulation made in the compositions of microbial community and its functions due to the interaction of GBNMs (Tang et al., 2021). In a study, influence of graphene on the diversity of gut microbiome has been reported using mice model (Xie et al., 2016) (Fig. 5 ). In this study, four-week-old male mice were exposed to different doses of graphene for a period of 28 days. It was noticed that the exposure of graphene led to increased biodiversity of gut microbiome meanwhile, play a critical role to change their community. Moreover, three different concentration of graphene (1 μg/d, 10 μg/d and 100 μg/d) were exposed to mice and it was found that 1 μg/d dose of graphene exhibited greater influence on the gut microbiome as compared to other two higher doses. This observation could be attributed to the aggregation of graphene at higher concentration whereas, graphene induced oxidative stress and damages made in the cell membrane integrity might be the possible reasons which significantly influenced the gut microbiome. These results were further supported by probing the ratio of gram positive and gram negative bacteria under the exposure of graphene. Since, the outer membrane of gram-negative bacteria possessed greater tolerating capability towards the damages caused by graphene as compared to gram-positive bacteria, increased abundance of gram-negative bacteria (from 71.3% to 92.0%) were observed. Consequently, exposure of graphene was responsible to amplify the abundance and types of antibiotic resistance genes. These observations explored that the exposure of graphene may cause a potential health issues to living organisms (Xie et al., 2016).

Lahiani et al., investigated interaction of pristine graphene with intestinal bacterial strains (e.g. L. acidophilus, B. longum, and E. coli) in a dose and time dependent approach following in vitro model (Lahiani et al., 2019). It was noticed that growth of the bacterial strains were significantly influenced by the dose of pristine graphene (Fig. 6 ). In addition, fresh fecal samples of rats were also employed to analyze the interaction of graphene with diverse intestinal microbiome, where dose and exposure time were varied. It was found that during the initial exposure for a period of 3 h, both the aerobic and anaerobic bacterial strains exhibited a two-fold increase and showed independency on the dose of pristine graphene. Nevertheless, a continuous exposure (at 100 μg/ml concentration) for a period of 24 h showed about 120% decrease in the aerobic bacteria meanwhile no change in case of anaerobic bacteria could be noticed. Conclusion was made that the exposure time and dose of graphene both exerted a significance influence on the population of bacterial strains. What's more, exposure of graphene to the fecal samples explored a prominent variation in taxonomic groups, and species. On the other hand, exposure of graphene was also responsible to raise short-chain fatty acid (SCFA) and butyric acid which resulted in improved abundance of butyrate-producing bacterial strains. Thus, it has been suggested that the exposure of graphene with a concentration 100 μg/ml can make worse effects on the intestinal microbiome and this may lead to several health issues (Lahiani et al., 2019).

Jia et al., studied the consequences of chronic exposure of GO on the health conditions of adult zebrafish (Jia et al., 2019). In their experiments, these authors probed the effect of varying concentration of GO on the growth of the intestinal microbiome and metabolic response in case of both, female and male zebrafish. It was found that the GO exert a significant variation on the diversity and abundance of bacterial community on the phylum and genus levels which also demonstrated the sex dependent differences made by GO exposure. More importantly, GO exposure was more severe in case of male zebrafish, leading greater damages of gut tissues along with inflammatory responses. In addition, in vitro study explored that the GO exhibited acute toxicity towards the survival capacity of the intestinal bacterial strains of zebrafish. On the other hand, expression level of pro-inflammatory genes could be induced due to the exposure of GO whereas, anti-inflammatory genes were found to be inhibited. These observations were more prominent in case of male zebrafish. Thus, GO may cause a potential effect on the inflammation and the perturbation of intestinal microbiome. These effects may be highly dependent on the metabolic and physiological activities in living organisms (Jia et al., 2019).

Dietary exposure of graphene based materials may be seen in terms of changes observed in gut function which is directly or indirectly associated with metabolic response of gut microbiota (Chen et al., 2019). In addition, at the early stage of exposure, deregulation in the energy budget may occur. In a study, physiological response to the dietary exposure of GO has been investigated in house crickets through in vivo approach (Seyed Alian et al., 2021). It was noticed that GO exposure at a concentration of 20 μg/g food could trigger an early stress meanwhile disturbance in the energy budget was recorded. Additionally, it was proposed that the alteration found in the physiological parameters may be associated with the hormesis effect due to the GO exposure in the diet of crickets (Seyed Alian et al., 2021). Gao et al., investigated the effect of GO on intestinal epithelial cells (IEC) following in vitro approach meanwhile, colitis through in vivo technique using mouse model was studied (Gao et al., 2021). It was noticed that, when GO was exposed to NCM460 cells for varying dose and time period, it showed toxic effects and also promoted inflammation. Besides, GO was responsible for the lysosomal dysfunction and obstructed the autophagy flux. On the other hand, in case of in vivo study, firstly mice were treated with dextran sulfate sodium (DSS) to induce colitis. Furthermore, when GO (60 mg/kg dose) was fed to mice through oral route; DSS-induced colitis was enhanced. These effects were seen in terms of shortened colon and ruthless pathological changes, along with induced autophagy (Gao et al., 2021). Such toxic impact of GO on the intestinal tract was also reported particularly in case of preexisting IBD by Liu et al. (S. Liu et al., 2021). It was found that the oral administration of GO made acute colitis more severe, induced by DSS exposure in mice. Further, mechanisms including activation of reactive oxygen species (ROS)/AMP-activated protein kinase (AMPK)/p53 pathway were involved, which lead GO-exacerbated DSS-induced inflammation and apoptosis in FHC cell line. Thus, GO exposure may exert a significant influence to alter the intestinal environment and affects the gut microbiome, as a consequence IBD in organisms can be appeared. Effect of different graphene derivatives on the dietary exposure using zebrafish as a model has been reported by Zheng et al. (2019). In this approach, graphene derivatives including GO, RGO and monolayer graphene powder (MLGP) were employed to investigate the influence on gut microbiota (Fig. 7 ). The results explored that in case of GO and RGO exposure there were no significant change that could be observed in the abundance and diversity of gut microbiota. Nevertheless, exposure of MLGP in diet caused a prominent change in the diversity of microbiota meanwhile no change could be recorded in their abundance (Fig. 3). Depending upon the nature of graphene derivatives, variation in the inflammatory response were also noticed. Besides, alteration in the intestinal morphology and the activities were also recorded which induced more vacuolation and generation of more goblet cells as compared to control system (Zheng et al., 2019). Thus, it was explored that exposure of graphene derivatives in diet may lead a potential threat to health owing to significant disruption made in the gut microbiota.

Interactions of GO and graphene nanoplatelets (GNPs) with the in vitro Caco-2/HT29 model of the intestinal barrier was investigated by Domenech et al. (2020). These authors did not observe any cyto-toxicological effects in the Caco-2/HT29 cells. In addition, no adverse effects on the integrity of the barrier or its permeability were recorded along with any oxidative damages. Nevertheless, a minute difference in the cytokines level could be noticed which explored a mild anti-inflammatory response due to the Interactions with GO and GNPs. On the other hand, cellular uptake (intestinal barrier model) particularly in case of GNPs was seen whereas, both GO and GNPs induced DNA damage. On the basis of obtained results, it was concluded that exposure of graphene based materials can lead moderate/severe risk to the intestinal barrier which may further influence the diversity of the microbiome (Domenech et al., 2020). Numerous studies have explored that gut microbiome dysbiosis can result in metabolic diseases, e.g. diabetes and allergies (Dedrick et al., 2020; Peroni et al., 2020). Significant changes in the diversity of gut microbiota are seen during the pregnancy (H. Yang et al., 2020). Such changes in the diversity of gut microbiota are likely to manage a number of phenomena (e.g. metabolic reactions, energy need, weight gain by mother and fetus) that take place in the body (Lundgren et al., 2018). On the other hand, alteration in gut microbiota can lead several issues e.g. preeclampsia and preterm birth along with abnormality in the function of placenta (Shrestha et al., 2019). Therefore, proper functioning of gut microbiota is quite essential for the successful pregnancy and to develop embryo-fetus. In this reference, to investigate the effect of GO on the possible alteration in gut microbiota diversity and the related complication during the pregnancy in mice were performed (X. Liu et al., 2021). In this approach, different dose of GO was exposed to mice through oral route during the gestational period. It was noticed that, in case of mice which showed failed pregnancy due to the exposure of GO, there was a significant change in gut microbiota. Further, compared to the control system GO-exposed gut microbiota exhibited reduced microbial specie number and community diversities. Moreover, dose dependent exposure of GO caused numerous complications related to pregnancy. These complications included resorbed embryo, skeletal development retardation, low weight of fetus, early or late dead of fetus, accompanying with placenta abnormalities. Thus, exposure of GO during pregnancy can significantly influence gut microbiota and lead several complications (X. Liu et al., 2021).

ce:section.ce:section-title: On the biocompatibility of GBNMs with gut microbiome
ce:section.ce:para: It is observed that the physicochemical properties of GBNMs may change when interact with different environment of a biological system (W. Yang et al., 2019). These factors may include temperature, pH, and chemical reagents which are likely to originate from the metabolic reactions of gut microbiome (Yang et al., 2021; Tai et al., 2014). In order to probe this fact, Guarnieri et al., studied the bio-transformational and biological effects of few layer graphene (FLGR) and GO following an in vitro human digestion assay (Guarnieri et al., 2018). The physicochemical properties of these GBNMs during the digestion process were further analyzed through Raman spectroscopy. Moreover, integrity and functionality of intestinal barrier were evaluated to determine the chronic exposure of FLGR and GO. It was found that, FLGR and GO exhibited aggregation due to the possible interactions with various environments of the GIT. Nevertheless, this phenomenon did not cause any structural change or degradation of nanostructures. Besides, integrity of the intestinal barrier was unaffected meanwhile no inflammation and cytotoxicity could be noticed with the chronic exposure. It was concluded that the FLGR and GO could be employed as biopersistent nanostructures when administered through the oral route. Thus, diversity of intestinal microbiome could be unaffected even when these nanostructures are likely to administrate through the oral route for different biotechnological applications (Guarnieri et al., 2018). Using in vitro model, interaction of GO with human intestinal bacterial strains were studied to investigate the antibacterial properties (Nguyen et al., 2015). It was found that exposure of GO even at varying concentrations (10 to 500 μg/ml) to both types of bacterial strains (gram positive and gram negative) did not show any toxic effects. On the other hand, GO exhibited mild cytotoxicity towards Caco-2 cells when exposed to 24 h, but concentration dependent cytotoxicity effects could not be observed. Bazina et al., also studied the cytotoxicity of graphene derivatives including GO, RGO, PRGO and graphene though in vitro model (Bazina et al., 2021). These graphene derivatives were employed to a 3-phase simulated digestion model, replicating biotransformations and likely to occur in the GIT after the ingestion. Further, analysis exposed a considerable agglomeration by all these materials owing to their interactions with the digestive proteins. As a result, a low cytotoxicity could be delivered by all graphene derivatives, but no severe effects were noticed in the exposed tricultures (small intestinal epithelium) model. Thus, it is suggested that the occasional ingestion of these materials in low quantity may not exert high cytotoxic. Nevertheless, possibility of the inflammatory or genotoxic effect when exposed for the short or long-term ingestion cannot be ignored and this may further perturb the gut microbiome and thus may lead to health issues (Bazina et al., 2021). Besides, physicochemical properties of graphene based materials such as their degree of oxidation may be helpful to support the growth of gut microbiota favorable to immune system (Henriques et al., 2020).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antibacterial effect of GBNMs favorable to gut microbiome
ce:section.ce:para: Modes of interaction of GBNMs with microorganisms can modulate the degree of antibacterial properties. To examine this fact, Lu et al., studied the effect of GO nanosheets alignment on the antibacterial properties towards E. coli (X. Lu et al., 2017). In this approach, magnetic field was applied to align GO nanosheets that lead to the formation of GO-film. It was noticed that when Escherichia coli interacted with vertically aligned GO-nanosheets with sharp edge features, showed improved antibacterial property as compared to the random or horizontal orientations. The vertically aligned GO film exhibited significantly lower cell viability than that of randomly oriented GO film and the Planar-GO film. In addition, in case of phospholipid vesicles (model system) it was observed that GO nanosheets induced physical distraction in lipid bilayer. Besides, a considerable oxidation of glutathione by GO was noticed owing to the direct electron-transferring. Thus, physicochemical mechanism both were responsible to penetrate the cell membrane, and therefore edge-based cytotoxic effect of GO could be noticed (X. Lu et al., 2017). Such antibacterial properties of GBNMs could be employed to treat food borne pathogen e.g. Listeria monocytogenes which are able cause dieses related to GIT microbiome (Davis et al., 2019).

Different physicochemical properties of graphene based materials including degree of oxidation, thickness and surface morphology may significantly contribute towards the interactions with biological systems (Zou et al., 2017). These facts have been well explored in the study of Henriques et al. (2020). In this study, these authors synthesized different types of graphene based films such as GO, few-layer GO (FLGO), RGO and few-layer graphene (FLGR). It was noticed that GO and FLGO were more oxidized, exhibited smoother and hydrophilic characteristics. On the other hand, RGO and FLGR were less/non-oxidized and exhibited relatively rougher and more hydrophobic properties. Further, all graphene based films could induce oxidation of glutathione and therefore, had potential to lead oxidative stress in biological systems. Besides, interactions of different bacterial strains with graphene based film were further studied. It was noticed that S. epidermidis, S. aureus, P. aeruginosa, and E. coli exhibited lower adherence towards GO and FLGO films. On the other hand, these bacterial strains exhibited better adherence and viability towards RGO and FLGR based films. Thus, by tuning the physicochemical properties of GBNMs such as degree of oxidation which does not favor the adhesion of bacterial strains along with non-cytotoxicity to the mammalian cells can be promising for different biomedical applications including antibacterial reagents to tackle pathogenic bacterial strains related to gut microbiome (Henriques et al., 2020).

Bacterial pathogens e.g. L. monocytogenes and S. enteric are known to be food borne pathogen (Gu et al., 2021). These bacterial strains can colonize the GIT of a number of hosts, including humans (Han et al., 2021). Antibacterial properties of GO, RGO and pristine graphene (PG) have been investigated against these bacterial strains (Kurantowicz et al., 2015). It was found that all these graphene based materials exhibited good antibacterial properties despite variation in cell wall structure of bacterial strains. Under the high concentration (250 μg/ml), growth of both the bacterial strains could be completely restricted. Further, it was noticed that in case of PG and RGO, bacterial strains could attach on the edge whereas in case of GO attachment was made on the surface. Moreover, among all these graphene based materials, GO exhibited the highest antibacterial property even at low concentration (25 μg/ml) (Kurantowicz et al., 2015). Such diverse physicochemical properties of GBNMs may be further exploited for the controlled growth of bacterial strains favorable to gut microbiome, meanwhile growth of the pathogenic bacterial strains could be restricted (Mulvey et al., 2018).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Gut microbiome modulation through GBNMs and their consequence
ce:section.ce:para: Hyperlipidemia (high cholesterol) covers several disorders in the body which result from a disordered lipid metabolism (Álvarez Ramírez et al., 2020). The hyperlipidemia has become an epidemic around the world which causes cardiovascular and nonalcoholic fatty liver disease (Ezeh and Ezeudemba, 2021). Further it has been reported that dysregulation of gut microbiota can be associated with hyperlipidemia (Tong et al., 2018). In this reference, capability of GO to modulate gut microbiota structure and blood/liver lipids have been investigated by Li et al. (Li et al., 2018). In this approach, high-fat diet induced hyperlipidemic mice was employed as the in vivo model to analyze the effect of GO where, GO was administrated through oral route for 4 weeks. It was noticed that ingestion of GO did not reduce the total number of gut bacteria. Meanwhile, GO played a significant role to elevate abundance of short-chain fatty acid (SCFA)-producing bacterial strains. This increased abundance of SCFA producing bacterial strain can play a potential role to improve the host metabolism. The real time PCR measurements explored that GO played a vital role to modulate gut microbiota. Furthermore, GO could significantly reduce the lipid levels in blood and liver. Thus, GO can be exploited to modulate SCFA-producing gut microbes, selectively. Therefore, GO can be of great importance to modulate the host's metabolism which is beneficial to the health of organisms. Size of graphene based materials also plays a significant role to influence the biodiversity of gut flora. To explore this fact, effect of carbon-14 labeled few layer graphene (CL-FLG) and smaller (S-FLG) on the biodiversity of gut flora have been investigated (K. Lu et al., 2017) (Fig. 8 ). It was found that the exposure of CL-FLG and S-FLG did not cause any significant change in the community richness as compared to control system. However, exposure of S-FLG could increase the community diversity whereas no significant difference was recorded in case of CL-FLG and control system. In addition, a quantitative analysis on the effect of these graphene based materials on gut microbial community structure was investigated in zebrafish. Difference in microbial community structure was recorded due to the exposure S-FLG as compared to control system. On the other hand no difference could be recorded in case of CL-FLG compared to control system. These observations explored that exposure of GBNMs varying in size can influence the microbial community structure and therefore, may be exploited to modulate the gut microbiome (K. Lu et al., 2017).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Microbiota friendly GBNMs as the possible therapeutic agent
ce:section.ce:para: The gut microbiota plays an important role to define the states of health and diseases of organism. It is well known that the perturbation in gut microbiome can be associated with various IBD dieses. In this context, preservation of gut-microbiome balance may be an indispensable way of treatment. In a recent study, potential of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as an alternative to remedy has been investigated (Lee et al., 2021). In this study, GQDs were orally administered to mice in order to treat the DSS-induced colitis. Further, therapeutic capability of GQDs was analyzed by measuring the changes in body weight, disease activity index along with colon length. In addition, influence of GQDs with varying dose on microbiota was investigated by using BL21 E. coli as a model system. Results explored that, compared to GO which showed decreased bacterial viability, no significant toxicological effect could be recorded in case of GQDs treatment in vitro and in vivo. It was concluded that oral administration of GQDs did not exert any negative influence on intestinal cells and gut-microbiota. More importantly symptoms of colitis e.g. loss of body weight and the secretion of inflammatory cytokines could be resolved owing to repetitive orally administrated GQDs. What's more, intestinal inflammation was also suppressed with the treatment GQDs. This study suggests that GQDS can be of great potential to be used as an alternative to microbiota-friendly treatment approach for colitis (Lee et al., 2021).

Chen et al., studied interaction of GO with different types of common human gut bacteria (Chen et al., 2014). The bacterial strains including B. adolescentis, L. acidophilus, E. coli, E. faecalis, and S. aureus were employed to analyzed the effect of GO. It was observed that the presence of GO in the bacterial media formed effective, anaerobic membrane scaffolds. This anaerobic membrane scaffolds were helpful to improve the antagonistic activity of B. adolescentis against the pathogens including E. coli and S. aureus. Moreover, GO could promote the proliferation of gut bacteria, particularly B. adolescentis and demonstrated the biocompatibility. Conclusion was made that the GO sheets can be employed as drug carriers to treat gut microbiota based diseases (Chen et al., 2014). GBNMs can also be of much importance to develop carriers for the encapsulation of probiotics and bioreactor packing. Using gelatin-GO nanocomposite, probiotic cell viability can be significantly improved and this can be further helpful to treat gut microbiota based diseases more effectively (Patarroyo et al., 2021). Besides, in view of various biological applications of GBNMs, it is imperative to analyze the growth kinetics of bacterial strains in presence of GBNMs accurately. The accurate analysis of bacterial growth is of much importance to explore the impacts of GBNMs in terms of their antibacterial, toxicological and biocompatibility impacts. This may further be helpful to accurately measure the relative changes in number of bacteria. As a result, it is possible to estimate how exposure stress can modulate the bacterial function. Yang et al., reported a DNA-based quantification (DBQ) technique to probe the growth kinetics of bacterial culture in presence of different dose of GO (Q. Yang et al., 2020). Such DBQ technique could be a potential approach and advantageous as compared to other methods (e.g. optical density, colony counting assay, automated image analysis, series of dilutions and flow cytometry) to investigate the growth of gut microbe in presence of GO. Further, suitability of this technique was investigated towards the quantification of bacterial strains in presence of GO using bare bacterial culture and human gut microbiome following in vitro method. The limit of detection could be estimated ~0.19 as OD600. It was suggested that the developed method could also be promising for the metabolite or protein analysis which is likely to be helpful to investigate the interaction of GBNMs with microbiome and their health consequence (Q. Yang et al., 2020). Additionally, few studies have been reported to evaluate the interactions of ENMs with bacterial strains via molecular dynamic simulation (MDS) to explore the possible mechanism behind the antibacterial activity (Y. Chen et al., 2020) and mode of interactions with the outer membrane of bacterial strains (Capeletti et al., 2019). Therefore, theoretical investigations can further be helpful to correlate the experimental studies on the interaction of GBNMs with gut microbiome, and this may likely to advance better understanding on the possible health consequences e.g. cancer, obesity, Type II diabetes, celiac diseases may be understand in a better way (Dahiya and Renuka, 2017).

The increasing use of GBNMs for variety of application has raised serious concerns about their exposure to human and living organisms. On the basis of several studies discussed in the present review, it may be concluded that GBNMs have enormous potential to alter the composition, diversity and function of gut microbiome up to a great extent. In addition, exposure of GBNMs led damages of gut tissues along with potential inflammatory effects. Since, gut microbiome play a potential role to manage the overall immune system, physiological processes and GI homeostasis; perturbed gut microbiome were responsible for the enteric disorder with the exposure of GBNMs. Studies have explored that perturbed gut microbiome can lead severe health consequences in organisms. IEC colitis, lysosomal dysfunction, inflammation, shortened colon and ruthless pathological changes and IBD like symptoms were also noticed due to the exposure of GBNMs. Besides, disorders including resorbed embryo, skeletal development retardation, and low weight of fetus, early or late dead of fetus were recorded in mice with the oral exposure of GBNMs, and these could be associated with the dysbiosis of gut microbiome and other physiological changes occurred in GIT. Notably, in most of the in vivo studies toxicological effects of GBNMs have been observed in case of healthy organisms which could be more severe in case of preexisting intestinal inflammation, and this might be attributed to dysbiosis of microbiome. Further, alterations in gut microbiome with GBNMs exposure were also recorded in dose and time dependent manner. Moreover, types of graphene derivative (graphene, GO, RGO, PRGO) and their physicochemical properties (e.g. mono layer, few layer and multi layer graphene) exert a significant role to alter the gut microbiome.

Nevertheless, contradictory results have been also reported on gut microbiome, intestinal immunity and GIT integrity. This may be correlated with several inconsistencies e.g. differences of in vivo model and other parameters e.g. dose and time dependent exposure, mode of exposure along with distinct physicochemical properties of graphene derivatives. In addition, adapted methodology to analyze gut microbiome is another key point which should be taken into the consideration and its overall impact on the health status of organism. In this context, a uniform model should be stabilized to explore the impact of ENMs on gut microbiome critically. Additionally, drawbacks of the currently employed animal models are other key issues when analyzing the impact of ENMs on the gut microbiome of human and its health consequences. This is mainly because of the significant difference in gut microbiome of human and other animals in terms of species, genus and physiological functions of GIT segments.

On the other hand, few studies have explored that exposure of GBNMs could be helpful to resolve some of the issues e.g. symptoms of colitis, loss of body weight and the secretion of inflammatory cytokines. Additionally, GO can exhibit antagonistic impact for the bacterial strains favorable to gut microbiome and against the pathogenic bacterial strains. Exposure of GO could also modulate the gut microbiome that may increase the abundance of SCFA producing bacterial strains and this may further be useful to overcome the risk of hyperlipidemia. Nevertheless, more intense and rigorous studies should be explored through in vivo models to establish critical understanding on the exposure of GBNMs with gut microbiome and the related health risks along with possible therapeutic strategies. Besides, to improve the scientific understandings on the interaction of gut microbiome with GBNMs along with critical knowledge about the possible mechanisms, more studies should be focused through the molecular dynamic simulation (MDS) approach. Such MDS approach is likely to be helpful to evaluate the potential health consequences of ENMs interaction with gut microbiome when analyze through in vivo models.

Farkad Bantun: Writing - original draft; Rajeev Singh: Conceptualization, Writing - original draft; Mustfa F. Alkhanani: Review and editing; Atiah H. Almalki: Review and editing; Freah Alshammary: Review and editing; Saif Khan: Review and editing; Shafiul Haque: Review and editing.
Manish Srivastava: Conceptualization, Writing - original draft, Review and editing.
